summary: Gruber explains why Digg's new DiggBar feature is harmful to the web and how he blocked it from Daring Fireball. The DiggBar frames other websites' content under Digg-branded URLs instead of redirecting users, effectively hijacking URLs and breaking the fundamental architecture of the web. Gruber compares this to the widely condemned frameset practices of the mid-1990s. He shares his PHP-based solution that detects DiggBar referrer URLs and serves a special message instead of page content. He dismisses Digg's traffic as low-quality and argues there's no legitimate justification for this kind of URL hijacking.
key_points: The DiggBar loads other sites inside a Digg-branded frame rather than redirecting, keeping the URL as digg.com instead of the actual destination
URL framing was broadly condemned in the mid-'90s when framesets first appeared, and the DiggBar is no different in principle
URLs are foundational to the web — they tell users where they are and provide something to bookmark and share
Gruber used PHP referrer checking with a regex to detect DiggBar URLs and block them by serving alternate content
Digg's traffic, while high-volume, is dismissed as low-quality and not worth accommodating
money_quotes: This, of course, is total bullshit.
URLs are the building block of the Web. They tell the user where they are. They give you something to bookmark to go back or to share with others.
The DiggBar breaks that, and I've seen no argument that makes it any more sense to support this than it does to support 1996-style frameset site embedding.
Digg sends a tremendous amount of traffic to sites that make it to the top of their front page, but it's the worst kind of traffic: mindless, borderline illiterates.
key_insight: Framing other sites' content under your own URL is a fundamental violation of how the web works, regardless of whether it's dressed up as a modern toolbar or a 1990s frameset.
